creative_brief: Hero image: founder standing in a farm field at golden hour, looking directly at camera — honest, unglamorous, real. Below the fold, tight close-up photography of actual FarmThru products (Bundarra pork, Collins seafood, Paris Creek dairy) with farm-name labels visible. Earthy palette — no supermarket gloss. Typography in sentence case throughout, generous whitespace, single-column layout to maintain the velvet-slide reading rhythm.
headline: I built a grocery store because I couldn't look my farmer in the eye
subhead: I'd been buying food for twenty years without once asking who grew it, how they grew it, or whether they could pay their bills. That's not a supply chain. That's wilful ignorance. So I started FarmThru.
hero_copy: Here's what I couldn't unsee: the average supermarket item travels over 2,400km before it reaches you. It passes through warehouses, wholesalers, and cold storage — each one skimming margin from the farmer and freshness from your food. I visited Rachel Ward's regenerative farm in Kempsey, NSW. She showed me soil she'd spent years restoring. And she told me what the big chains actually pay producers. I couldn't sleep that night. Not because of the numbers — because I'd never bothered to ask.

sections.heading: The confession that became a company
sections.body:
I didn't set out to start a grocery store. I set out to fix my own shopping. I wanted to know the name of the farmer behind every item in my kitchen. Not a logo. Not a brand story on a packet. An actual person.

So I called them. Bundarra Farm — they raise pork in NSW and make their own salami and ham. Collins Wild Caught — wild-caught seafood off the South Coast. Paris Creek Farms — carbon-neutral biodynamic dairy from Meadows, SA. Farmer Brown — eggs from NSW. Little Yarran Farm.

Every single one said the same thing: the current system doesn't work for us. Zero warehouses. Zero wholesalers. Zero middlemen. That became the model. FarmThru ships direct from farm to our Brookvale hub. You order online, collect Monday to Friday. Fresher food. Fairer farmer pay. No pretending someone else is checking.

sections.heading: This isn't a subscription box — it's a grocery store you can trust
sections.body:
No lock-in contracts. No weekly boxes of vegetables you didn't choose. FarmThru is an online grocery store stocked entirely from regenerative farms. Order what you want, when you want. Collect from our Brookvale hub Monday to Friday.

We'll show you where your food comes from. Not a region. Not a country of origin in fine print. The actual farm, the actual farmer, the actual practice. Because once you see the difference between soil that's been restored and soil that's been stripped, you can't go back to not caring.

Only food we'd feed our own. That's the rule. If it doesn't meet it, it doesn't make the store.

sections.heading: Own a piece of what we're building
sections.body:
I started FarmThru because I wanted to fix how my family eats. But this is bigger than one family. If you believe grocery shopping should support the people who grow your food — not extract from them — you can be part of it.

We're opening our equity crowdfunding campaign so everyday people can own a piece of FarmThru. Not just shop with us. Partner with us. Vote for change with your wallet and your ownership.

Join the waitlist now with a fully refundable $5 deposit to be first in when the campaign opens. No commitment beyond that. You'll get early access and full details before anyone else.

Investing in early-stage ventures carries real risk, including the possibility of losing your entire investment. This isn't a guaranteed return — it's a bet on a better food system. Read the offer document carefully before deciding.

sections.heading: Compliance
sections.body: FarmThru's equity crowdfunding campaign is conducted in accordance with Australian law. Investment in early-stage companies is speculative and carries a high degree of risk, including the potential loss of your entire investment. Past performance is not indicative of future results. No returns are projected or guaranteed. You should read the offer document in full and consider seeking independent financial advice before making any investment decision. The $5 waitlist deposit is fully refundable at any time.
